content:
Hi, I'm working on the add a tracepoint for Arc::drop when refcount reaches 0: https://github.com/fbq/linux-rust/commit/c36d185e728eace05221152ee1596d99f384f2a3

The reason is that Arc::drop sometimes could be expensive, and you don't want it in your fastpath, so step 0 is to find it out. And a tracepoint means perf and bpf get a chance to step into and do their work.

One thing I could use some advise is about the tracepoint data, right now I'm using the core::any::type_name so that users could figure out the inner data type, and maybe put a filter on. But that also means more space for kernel text. I wonder whether I could use core::any::TypeId and somehow retrieve the type name with TypeId from debug information?

Anyway, going to send it out later this week for broader review, let me know if you want to be Cced ;-)

I wonder whether I could use core::any::TypeId and somehow retrieve the type name with TypeId from debug information?

No, rustc doesn't record any debug info to map from TypeId back to the type name.
By the way the exact result of type_name is not guaranteed to be stable. Does linux provide any stability guarantees for tracepoints?

bjorn3 said:

By the way the exact result of type_name is not guaranteed to be stable. Does linux provide any stability guarantees for tracepoints?

Interesting point. I guess the answer is no, since it's just a type name, but C doesn't have template, so right now Linux doesn't have a "generic" tracepoint, I think.

I don't know the current feeling, but this 2009 article talked about tracepoints as ABI: https://lwn.net/Articles/330402/

A tracepoint exists to make specific kernel information available to user space. So, in some real sense, it becomes part of the kernel ABI. As an ABI feature, a tracepoint becomes set in stone once it's shipped in a stable kernel. There is not a universal agreement on the immutability of kernel tracepoints, but the simple fact is that, once these tracepoints become established and prove their usefulness, changing them will cause user-space tracing tools to break. That means that, even if tracepoints are not seen as a stable ABI the way system calls are, there will still be considerable resistance to changing them.
(I used to be involved from the SystemTap side)
that said, type_name as a parameter seems fine to me; the tracepoint itself would not change

bjorn3 said:

I wonder whether I could use core::any::TypeId and somehow retrieve the type name with TypeId from debug information?

No, rustc doesn't record any debug info to map from TypeId back to the type name.

But can we make that happen? It doesn't seem like something wrong ;-)

Josh Stone (cuviper) said:

that said, type_name as a parameter seems fine to me; the tracepoint itself would not change

Right, tracepoint itself cannot change, but its parameter certainly doesn't need to be the same between builds, thanks for the extra confirmation ;-)

Boqun Feng said:

bjorn3 said:

I wonder whether I could use core::any::TypeId and somehow retrieve the type name with TypeId from debug information?

No, rustc doesn't record any debug info to map from TypeId back to the type name.

But can we make that happen? It doesn't seem like something wrong ;-)

Or ,TypeId is just unique per CU?

Creating a section with TypeId -> type name mapping would be possible, however I don't think having a function in the standard library for the lookup would work as wasm doesn't support merging the section from all cgus and giving this function the start and end location of the section. It would also need OS integration in case of dylibs. As for exposing the section as a stable feature to lookup yourself, that won't work either. We likely want to change the representation of TypeId and thus this section in the future to avoid all hash collisions. For example my making it a pointer to the mangled type name instead of a plain u64.
